skill.name: draft-commit
skill.description: Draft clear commit messages by analyzing staged Git changes and applying configurable tone and format options.
skill.summary: Generate reviewable commit message drafts from staged Git diffs.
skill.author: AbeJitsu

security_audit.summary: Static weak-cryptography and reconnaissance detections were reviewed and dismissed as false positives from ordinary prose and examples. The confirmed residual risk is the intended use of Git commands, especially reading staged diffs that may contain sensitive project content. No evidence found of malicious intent, network exfiltration, destructive repository changes, or prompt injection attempts.

medium_findings.title: Staged Diff Inspection Uses External Git Command
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally uses git diff --cached to inspect staged repository changes. This is legitimate for commit drafting, but staged diffs can contain secrets, proprietary code, or sensitive data that will enter the assistant context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly documented and directly supports the skill purpose. The risk is contextual data exposure, not command injection or malicious execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain metadata, configuration names, prose, or commit message examples. No cryptographic algorithm implementation, password hashing, encryption code, or security-sensitive digest use was found in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found only documentation and JSON settings at the flagged lines. There is no code path that performs cryptography.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The flagged line describes reviewing which files changed in a staged Git diff. It does not enumerate system information, environment variables, host details, or network configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section explains commit-message analysis. The text is about repository change summaries, not system reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Commit Commands Are User-Controlled Examples
low_findings.description: Several command examples show git add, git commit, and git reset commands for the user to run manually. The skill states that it does not stage changes, commit automatically, or modify the repository.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are presented as user instructions and the documentation explicitly says the skill does not modify the repository. No automatic execution wrapper is present in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Git Diff Reads Local Repository Content
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on git diff --cached to read staged changes. Users should review staged content first because the diff may include secrets or private code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is clearly present and intentionally reads local repository data. No evidence found that it sends data to an external endpoint beyond normal assistant processing.

content.value_statement: Commit messages often lose important context after code changes are staged. This skill reviews staged Git changes and drafts a clear message for human approval.
actual_capabilities: Reviews staged Git changes with git diff --cached.
Infers whether changes are features, fixes, refactors, tests, documentation, chores, style, or performance work.
Drafts descriptive or conventional commit messages.
Can include file counts, line counts, and context when configured.
Shows the message for review instead of committing automatically.
limitations: It only analyzes changes that are already staged.
It does not create commits or stage files for the user.
It may miss intent that is not visible in the diff.
Sensitive staged content may be included in the assistant context.

use_cases.title: Prepare review-ready commits
use_cases.description: Turn staged code changes into a clear message before opening a pull request.

use_cases.title: Standardize commit style
use_cases.description: Help contributors produce consistent descriptive or conventional commit messages.

use_cases.title: Summarize documentation updates
use_cases.description: Create concise commit messages for staged guide, README, or setup changes.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a basic commit message
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this after staging a small, focused change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use draft-commit to review my staged changes and suggest one clear commit message.

prompt_templates.title: Use conventional commit format
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this when the repository follows conventional commits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes and draft a conventional commit message with the best type and scope.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the reason for the change
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this when the commit needs helpful context for reviewers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my staged changes and draft a commit message that explains what changed and why it matters.

prompt_templates.title: Compare message options
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this for mixed or higher-impact changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes, identify the main intent, and provide three commit message options with different levels of detail.

output_examples.input: Staged changes add tests and update validation behavior.
output_examples.output: Add validation coverage for account settings. Expands test coverage for required fields and clarifies expected error handling.

output_examples.input: Staged changes update README setup steps and deployment notes.
output_examples.output: docs: Clarify setup and deployment guidance. Updates contributor instructions so local setup and release steps are easier to follow.

output_examples.input: Staged changes refactor a service module and remove duplicated logic.
output_examples.output: Refactor service handling to share common logic. Reduces duplication and keeps request behavior consistent across related paths.

best_practices: Stage one logical change before running the skill.
Review the staged diff for secrets before asking for a draft.
Edit the suggested message when important context is missing from the diff.
anti_patterns: Do not use it on unrelated staged changes that need separate commits.
Do not accept the draft without checking accuracy.
Do not stage credentials, keys, or private data before analysis.

faq.question: Does this skill create the commit for me?
faq.answer: No. It drafts a message for review, and you run the final Git commit command yourself.

faq.question: Which changes does it analyze?
faq.answer: It analyzes staged changes only, using the same content shown by git diff --cached.

faq.question: Can it write conventional commits?
faq.answer: Yes. The configuration supports conventional commit style with common types like feat, fix, docs, and refactor.

faq.question: Can I change the tone?
faq.answer: Yes. The included configuration supports supportive and concise message styles.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use care. Staged diffs may contain private code or secrets, so review the staged content first.

faq.question: What should I do if the draft is wrong?
faq.answer: Edit the message manually or rerun the skill after staging a smaller, clearer set of changes.
